Recurrent neural networks, long short-term memory [13] and gated recurrent [7] neural networks in particular, have been firmly established as state of the art approaches in sequence modeling and transduction problems such as language modeling and machine translation [35, 2, 5].

question: What limitations or unresolved issues are reported? Paper: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for
gold_answer:
The ma- jor limitation is that standard language models are unidirectional, and this limits the choice of archi- tectures that can be used during pre-training.
Critically, however, the BERT Transformer uses bidirectional self-attention, while the GPT Trans- former uses constrained self-attention where every token can only attend to context to its left.4
However, in prior work, only sentence embeddings are transferred to down-stream tasks, where BERT transfers all pa- rameters to initialize end-task model parameters.

To characterize language model scaling we train a wide variety of models, varying a number of factors including:
• Model size (ranging in size from 768 to 1.5 billion non-embedding parameters)
• Dataset size (ranging from 22 million to 23 billion tokens)
• Shape (including depth, width, attention heads, and feed-forward dimension)

question: What method or technical approach does the paper propose? Paper: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
gold_answer:
Our basic pre-training approach, including model, data, and training, is similar to the process described in [RWC+19], with relatively straightforward scaling up of the model size, dataset size and diversity, and length of training.
• Fine-Tuning (FT) has been the most common approach in recent years, and involves updating the weights of a pre-trained model by training on a supervised dataset speciﬁc to the desired task.
• Few-Shot (FS) is the term we will use in this work to refer to the setting where the model is given a few demonstrations of the task at inference time as conditioning [RWC+19], but no weight updates are allowed.
• One-Shot (1S) is the same as few-shot except that only one demonstration is allowed, in addition to a natural language description of the task, as shown in Figure 1.
required_claims: Our basic pre-training approach, including model, data, and training, is similar to the process described in [RWC+19], with relatively straightforward scaling up of the model size, dataset size and diversity, and length of training.
• Fine-Tuning (FT) has been the most common approach in recent years, and involves updating the weights of a pre-trained model by training on a supervised dataset speciﬁc to the desired task.

• Few-Shot (FS) is the term we will use in this work to refer to the setting where the model is given a few demonstrations of the task at inference time as conditioning [RWC+19], but no weight updates are allowed.

question: What are the paper's main contributions? Paper: LLaMA: Open and Efﬁcient Foundation Language Models
gold_answer:
We introduce LLaMA, a collection of founda- tion language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters.
Unlike Chinchilla, PaLM, or GPT-3, we only use publicly available data, making our work com- patible with open-sourcing, while most existing models rely on data which is either not publicly available or undocumented (e.g.

question: Compare the main methods or contributions of papers 1706.03762 and 1810.04805.
gold_answer:
In this work we propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output.
We introduce a new language representa- tion model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers.
The contributions of our paper are as follows:
We introduce BERT and its detailed implementa- tion in this section.
In this section, we present BERT ﬁne-tuning re- sults on 11 NLP tasks.
